blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلطَّارِقِ (١)
By the heaven, and by the nightly visitor (1)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلطَّارِقُ (٢)
(and what will show you what is the nightly visitor? (2)
ٱلنَّجْمُ ٱلثَّاقِبُ (٣)
It is the brilliant star): (3)
إِن كُلُّ نَفْسٍ لَّمَّا عَلَيْهَا حَافِظٌ (٤)
there is a guard over every soul. (4)
فَلْيَنظُرِ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مِمَّ خُلِقَ (٥)
So let man consider from what he was created. (5)
خُلِقَ مِن مَّآءٍ دَافِقٍ (٦)
He was created from an effusing fluid (6)
يَخْرُجُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ ٱلصُّلْبِ وَٱلتَّرَآئِبِ (٧)
which issues from between the loins and the breast-bones. (7)
إِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ رَجْعِهِۦ لَقَادِرٌ (٨)
Indeed He is able to bring him back [after death], (8)
يَوْمَ تُبْلَى ٱلسَّرَآئِرُ (٩)
on the day when the secrets are examined (9)
فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن قُوَّةٍ وَلَا نَاصِرٍ (١٠)
and he shall have neither power nor helper. (10)

The Content of Surah At-Tariq and Its Virtue

The subjects of this Surah mainly revolve around two axes:

  1. The axis of the Hereafter and the Resurrection.
  2. The axis of the Holy Quran and its value and importance.

However, at the beginning, after thought-provoking oaths, it refers to the existence of Divine observers over man.

Then, to prove the possibility of the Resurrection, it points to the first life and the beginning of man's creation from a drop of fluid, concluding: The God who is capable of creating him from such an insignificant and worthless fluid has the power to bring him back again.

In the next stage, it points to some characteristics of the Day of Resurrection, then highlights the importance of the Quran by mentioning several meaningful oaths, and finally ends the Surah by threatening the disbelievers with Divine punishment.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, we read in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله:

"Whoever recites it, Allah will grant him ten good deeds for every star that exists in the sky!" [^1] And in a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is stated:

"Whoever recites Surah 'By the heaven and the night-comer' (At-Tariq) in his obligatory prayers will have a great rank and status before Allah on the Day of Resurrection, and will be among the companions of the Prophets and their friends in Paradise." [^2] It is obvious that it is the content of the Surah and acting upon it that brings about such a great reward, not a recitation devoid of action.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 469. [^2]: Thawab al-A'mal according to Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 549.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful.

By the heaven and the night-comer (1) And what can make you know what the night-comer is? (2) It is the piercing star (3) There is no soul but that it has over it a protector (4)

So let man observe from what he was created (5) He was created from a spurting water (6) Emerging from between the backbone and the ribs (7) Indeed, He, over his return, is Capable (8) The Day when secrets are tested (9)

Then he will have no power or any helper (10)

Commentary

O Man, Observe from What You Were Created!

This Surah, like many other Surahs in the last part of the Quran, begins with beautiful and thought-provoking oaths—oaths that serve as a prelude to the statement of a great reality.

"By the heaven and the night-comer!" (وَ السَّماءِ وَ الطَّارِقِ).

"And what can make you know what the night-comer is?" (وَ ما أَدْراكَ مَا الطَّارِقُ).

"It is a high, bright star that pierces the darkness" (النَّجْمُ الثَّاقِبُ).

Tariq is derived from the root tarq (on the weight of barq), meaning "to knock." A path is called tariq because it is "knocked" (trodden) by the feet of travelers, and mitraqah means a sledgehammer or hammer used for striking metals and the like.

Since house doors are closed at night, and anyone arriving at night must knock on the door, those who arrive at night are called tariq.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, says regarding the hypocrite Ash'ath bin Qays, who came to his door at night bringing a sweet halva, thinking in his vain imagination that he could win Ali's عليه السلام heart to rule in his favor in a certain matter:

"And more amazing than that is the story of one who knocked on our door (tariq) at night with a covered vessel full of delicious halva..." [^1] However, the Quran itself interprets Tariq here, saying: "This nocturnal traveler is that bright star that appears in the sky and is so high that it is as if it wants to pierce the ceiling of the sky, and its light is so dazzling that it pierces the darkness and penetrates the human eye" (Note that thaqib is from the root thaqb, meaning "to pierce").

Regarding whether a specific star is intended, such as the Pleiades (due to its height and distance in the sky), or Saturn, or Venus, or meteors (due to their dazzling brightness), or if it refers to all the stars in the sky, several interpretations have been mentioned.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 224.

However, considering that in the subsequent verses it is interpreted as al-Najm al-Thaqib (the piercing star), it becomes clear that it does not mean just any star, but rather bright stars whose light pierces the veils of darkness and penetrates the human eye.

In some narrations, al-Najm al-Thaqib has been interpreted as the star "Saturn," which is one of the planets of the solar system and is very brilliant and luminous.

This meaning is derived from a Hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, where an astronomer asked him what "the piercing" (al-thaqib) in Allah's words "the piercing star" means.

He replied: "It is the star Saturn, whose rising is in the seventh heaven, and its light pierces the heavens and reaches the lower heaven; therefore, Allah named it the piercing star." [^1] It is noteworthy that Saturn is the last and farthest planet of the solar system visible to the naked eye, and since it is placed in the seventh orbit relative to the Sun in the arrangement of the planets (counting the Moon's orbit), the Imam عليه السلام in this Hadith counted its orbit as the seventh heaven.

This star has characteristics that make it worthy of an oath; on one hand, it is the farthest observable planet of the solar system, and for this reason, in Arabic literature, anything high is likened to it, and it is sometimes called "the Sheikh of the Stars." [^2] The star Saturn, whose Persian name is Keyvan, has several luminous rings surrounding it and has eight "moons." The luminous rings of Saturn that surround it are among the most amazing celestial phenomena, about which astronomers have various opinions, and it remains a mysterious phenomenon.

Some believe Saturn has ten moons, eight of which can be seen with ordinary astronomical telescopes, and two others are visible only with very large telescopes. ^3 Of course, these secrets were not clear to anyone on the day the Quran was revealed; they became manifest later with the passing of centuries.

However, in any case, interpreting the piercing star specifically as "Saturn" may be by way of mentioning a prominent example, and it does not contradict interpreting it as other high and bright stars of the sky; we know that "exemplary interpretation" (tafsir misdaqi) is frequent in our narrations.

In verse 10 of Surah As-Saffat, we read: "Except him who snatches a fragment by stealth, and there follows him a piercing flame (shihabun thaqib)." In this verse, the "flame" (shihab) is described as "piercing" (thaqib), and since this celestial phenomenon is among the wonders of the creation, it could be one of the interpretations of the verse under discussion.

The occasion of revelation mentioned in some books of commentary for the above verse also supports this meaning. [^4]

Now let us see what these oaths are for. In the next verse, He says: "Indeed, every soul has over it a protector" (إِنْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ لَمَّا عَلَيْها حافِظٌ) [^5] who records, regulates, and preserves its deeds, keeping them for reckoning and recompense.

As we read in verses 10, 11, and 12 of Surah Al-Infitar: "And indeed, over you are keepers, noble and recording, they know whatever you do."

In this way, you are never alone; whoever you are and wherever you are, you are under the supervision of Divine angels and the agents of the Lord. This is a matter whose consideration is extremely effective in the reform and education of man.

It is noteworthy that the verse does not specify who this "protector" is or what matters they protect. However, other verses of the Quran clearly testify that the protectors are angels, and what they protect are the deeds of man, whether they be acts of obedience or sins.

The possibility has also been raised that it refers to protecting man from accidents and perils; truly, if Divine care were not exercised over humans, few would die a natural death, for the volume of accidents is so great that no one would escape them safely, especially children at a young age.

Or it could mean protecting man against the whispers of devils; if this Divine protection were not present, the whispers of the devils among jinn and men are so numerous that no one would remain safe from them.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 550 (Hadith 4). [^2]: Dehkhoda Encyclopedia, entry "Zuhal" (Saturn).

[^4]: Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 397. [^5]: "In" in this verse is the negative "in," and "lamma" means "illa" (except).

However, considering that the subsequent verses speak of the issue of the Hereafter and the reckoning of deeds, the first interpretation is the most appropriate of all, although combining all three interpretations in the noble verse does not seem far-fetched.

Interestingly, there is a living relationship between the oaths taken previously and the issue of the angels' supervision over human deeds, which serves as the response to the oath. For the high heaven and the stars that constantly traverse their regular paths are evidence of the existence of order and reckoning in this great universe; so how is it possible for human deeds to be without reckoning and for Divine observers not to supervise them?

Then, as an argument for the issue of the Resurrection against those who considered it impossible, He says: "So let man observe from what he was created!" (فَلْيَنْظُرِ الْإِنْسانُ مِمَّ خُلِقَ). In this way, the Quran takes the hand of all humans and returns them to their first creation, asking them with an interrogative sentence: "What was your creation from?"

And without waiting for their answer, He gives the answer to this question, which is clear, Himself, and adds: "He was created from a spurting water!" (خُلِقَ مِنْ ماءٍ دافِقٍ), which is a description of the male fluid in which the sperm is swimming and which spurts out at the time of exit.

Then, in another description of this water, He says: "Emerging from between the backbone and the ribs" (يَخْرُجُ مِنْ بَيْنِ الصُّلْبِ وَ التَّرائِبِ).

Sulb means the back, and tara'ib is the plural of taribah, which according to the famous view among linguists, refers to the bones of the upper chest, the place where a necklace rests. As stated in Lisan al-Arab: "All linguists said it is the place of the necklace on the chest."

At the same time, several other meanings have been mentioned for it, including that tara'ib means the front part of the human (as opposed to sulb which is the back), or that tara'ib means the hands, feet, and eyes, or the entire chest bones, or four ribs from the right and four from the left.

In any case, regarding what is meant by sulb and tara'ib in this noble verse, there is much discussion among commentators, and many interpretations have been given for it, including:

  1. Sulb refers to men and tara'ib refers to women, because men are the manifestation of firmness (salabat) and women are the manifestation of delicacy and beauty. Therefore, the verse refers to the composition of the human embryo from the male and female fluids, which in today's terminology are called "sperm" and "ovum."

  2. Sulb refers to the man's back, and tara'ib refers to his chest and the front parts of his body. Therefore, it means the male fluid that emerges from the internal parts of the abdomen located between the back and the front.

  3. It refers to the emergence of the fetus from the woman's womb, which is located between her back and the front parts of her body.

  4. Some have said this verse refers to a precise scientific point that recent discoveries have unveiled and which was certainly hidden from everyone at the time of the Quran's revelation. That is: the germ cells are taken from the male "testis" and the female "ovary," and studies by embryologists show that when these two first appear in the embryo, they are located adjacent to the kidneys, approximately opposite the middle of the spinal column between the sulb (back) and tara'ib (the lowest ribs). Then, with the passage of time and the growth of these two organs, they gradually descend from that location and each takes its current position. Since the creation of man is from the combination of the male and female fluids, and the original location of these two at the beginning is between the sulb and tara'ib, the Quran chose such an expression—an expression that was known to no one at that time and which modern embryology has unveiled. [^1] To put it more clearly, the male testis and the female ovary at the beginning of their formation—that is, when the man and woman themselves were in the embryonic stage—were located in their backs, approximately level with the middle of the spinal column, such that it can be said the sperm-producing apparatus of the man and the egg-producing apparatus of the woman were both between the sulb and tara'ib. But gradually, as the creation of the man and woman in the mother's womb becomes complete, they separate from there and gradually descend, such that at birth, the male testis is located outside the abdomen next to the genitals, and the female ovary is adjacent to the uterus.

However, the major problem with this interpretation is that the Quran says that spurting water emerges from between the sulb and tara'ib, meaning at the time of exit, the water passes between these two, whereas according to this interpretation, it is not so at the time of the fluid's exit; rather, the reproductive organs were between the sulb and tara'ib when they themselves were in the mother's womb. Furthermore, interpreting tara'ib as the last lower ribs is not without debate.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 30, p. 113.

  1. The meaning of this sentence is that the semen is actually taken from all parts of the human body, and therefore its exit is accompanied by a general excitement of the body, followed by a total weakness of the body. Thus, sulb and tara'ib refer to the entire back and the entire front of the human.

  2. Some have also said the main factor in the production of semen is the "spinal cord" in the man's back, and then the heart and liver, one of which is under the chest bones and the other between the two, and this is why the expression "between the sulb and tara'ib" was chosen for it.

But before anything else, one must pay attention to this important point to solve the problem: in the above verses, only the male fluid is being discussed, because the expression ma'in dafiq (spurting water) applies to the male fluid, not the female's, and it is to this that the pronoun in yakhruju (emerges) in the next verse refers, saying: "This spurting water comes out from between the sulb and tara'ib."

On this account, involving the woman in this Quranic discussion does not seem appropriate; rather, the most suitable interpretation is to say that the Quran has pointed to one of the two main components of the embryo, which is the male fluid and is perceptible to everyone, and the meaning of sulb and tara'ib is the back and front parts of the man, because the man's fluid emerges from between these two. [^1]

This is a clear interpretation, free from any complexity and consistent with what is found in lexicons regarding the meaning of these two words. At the same time, it is possible that a more important truth is hidden in this verse that has not been discovered for us at the level of today's science, and the discoveries of scientists in the future will unveil it.

Then, He draws a conclusion from this statement, saying: "The One who created man in the beginning from a drop of fluid is capable of bringing him back to life once more" (إِنَّهُ عَلى‌ رَجْعِهِ لَقادِرٌ). In the beginning, it was dust, and then after passing through stages, it became a drop of fluid, and the fluid, after passing through complex and amazing stages, turned into a complete human being; therefore, his return to life and a second existence poses no problem.

Similar statements are seen in other verses of the Quran, including verse 5 of Surah Al-Hajj: "O people! If you are in doubt about the Resurrection, then indeed, We created you from dust, then from a drop of fluid..."

And also in verse 67 of Surah Maryam, we read: "Does man not remember that We created him before, while he was nothing?"

In the next verse, He describes that great day, saying: "This return will be realized on the Day when secrets are tested" (يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ). [^2]

[^1]: In other verses of the Quran, when the creation of man from a drop of fluid is mentioned, the focus is mostly on the male fluid, which is a perceptible matter (see verses 46 of An-Najm and 37 of Al-Qiyamah). [^2]: "Yawm" (Day) here is an adverb of time and is related to "raj'ihi" (his return) in the previous verse.

Tubla is from the root balwa, meaning "to test" or "to examine," and since the reality of things becomes manifest and clear during a test, this root is used here in the sense of "appearance" and "manifestation."

Sara'ir is the plural of sarirah, meaning internal and hidden states, qualities, and intentions.

Yes, on that day, which is the "Day of Manifestation" and the "Day of Appearance," internal secrets will be revealed, whether they be faith, disbelief, hypocrisy, good or evil intentions, or ostentation and sincerity. This manifestation will be a source of pride and additional blessing for the believers, and for the criminals, a source of shame and the origin of humiliation and disgrace. How painful it is for a person to have hidden his internal ugliness from Allah's creation for a lifetime and lived with honor among people, only to be ashamed and disgraced before all creatures on that day when all hidden secrets are revealed—sometimes the pain and suffering of this punishment is more painful than the fire of Hell.

In verse 41 of Ar-Rahman, it is also stated: "The criminals will be known by their marks." And in other verses, it is mentioned that on the Day of Resurrection, "some faces will be white and bright, and some faces will be dark and dust-stained" (Abasa 38-41).

Indeed, just as the Tariq and the stars appear in the sky at night and emerge from concealment, the protectors and observers who are commissioned to protect and record human deeds will also make everything manifest there.

In a narration from Mu'adh bin Jabal, it is reported that he said: I asked the Messenger of Allah about the interpretation of sara'ir in this noble verse, and I said: "What are the hidden secrets with which Allah will test the servants in the Hereafter?" He said:

"Your secrets are your deeds, such as prayer, fasting, zakat, wudu, the ghusl of janabah, and every obligatory act; for all deeds are in reality hidden secrets. If a man wishes, he says 'I have prayed' while he has not prayed, and if he wishes, he says 'I have performed wudu' while he has not performed wudu. And that is the meaning of Allah's words: 'The Day when secrets are tested.'" [^1]

But the major problem on that day is that "for man, there is no power from within, nor any helper from without" (فَما لَهُ مِنْ قُوَّةٍ وَ لا ناصِرٍ): No power to cast a veil over the ugliness of his deeds and intentions, and no helper to rescue him from Divine punishment.

This meaning appears in many verses of the Quran: that on that day, there is neither friend nor helper, no ransom or sacrifice is accepted, no way of escape or return is placed before man, and no path to flee from the grasp of the Lord's justice. The only and absolute means of salvation is faith and righteous deeds; yes, the source of salvation is only this.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 472; a similar meaning is also found in Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 336.

Ayahs

وَٱلسَّمَآءِ ذَاتِ ٱلرَّجْعِ (١١)
By the resurgent heaven, (11)
وَٱلْأَرْضِ ذَاتِ ٱلصَّدْعِ (١٢)
and by the furrowed earth: (12)
إِنَّهُۥ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ (١٣)
it is indeed a conclusive discourse, (13)
وَمَا هُوَ بِٱلْهَزْلِ (١٤)
and not a jest. (14)
إِنَّهُمْ يَكِيدُونَ كَيْدًا (١٥)
Indeed they are devising a stratagem, (15)
وَأَكِيدُ كَيْدًا (١٦)
and I [too] am devising a plan. (16)
فَمَهِّلِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَمْهِلْهُمْ رُوَيْدًۢا (١٧)
So respite the faithless; give them a gentle respite. (17)

Commentary

I Shall Nullify the Enemies' Plots!

Following the previous verses, which argued for the issue of Resurrection by drawing attention to the initial creation of man from a drop of fluid, these verses continue the discussion to emphasize the matter of Resurrection and point to some other evidences, stating: "By the sky which returns [rain]" (وَ السَّماءِ ذاتِ الرَّجْعِ).

"And by the earth which cracks open [with sprouting plants]" (وَ الْأَرْضِ ذاتِ الصَّدْعِ).

"Indeed, it is a decisive word" that you shall be brought back to life (إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ).

It is a serious word "and it is not a jest!" (وَ ما هُوَ بِالْهَزْلِ).

"Raj‘" (رجع) is derived from the root "ruju‘" (رجوع), meaning "return." The Arabs refer to rain as "raj‘" because the water that rose from the earth and the seas returns to the earth via clouds and rain, or because the falling of rain repeats at various intervals.

Hollows in which rainwater collects are also called "raj‘," either because of the collection of rainwater in them or because of the waves that appear on their surface due to the blowing of the breeze [1].

[1]: Mufradat al-Raghib, under the root "raj‘".

"Sad‘" (صدع) means a crack or split in solid and hard bodies. In light of what was said regarding the meaning of "raj‘," it is understood here as a reference to the cracking of dry and hard lands after the descent of rain and the growth and emergence of plants.

In reality, these two oaths are an allusion to the revival of dead lands by means of rain, which the Quran has repeatedly mentioned as a proof for the issue of Resurrection, such as in verse 11 of Surah Qaf: وَ أَحْيَيْنا بِهِ بَلْدَةً مَيْتاً كَذلِكَ الْخُرُوجُ—"And We give life thereby to a dead land; thus is the resurrection [from the graves]!" Thus, there is a clear correspondence between these oaths and the subject for which the oath is taken. This is one of the subtleties of Quranic eloquence, where a striking harmony is observed between the oaths and the matter being sworn upon.

Or to put it another way: just as in verse 5 of Surah al-Hajj, when arguing for the issue of Resurrection, it utilizes both the argument of the creation of man from a drop of fluid and the stages of the embryo, as well as the argument of the revival of dead lands through the descent of rain; in this Surah (Surah al-Tariq), both issues are likewise emphasized.

Some have mentioned another interpretation for the phrase "وَ السَّماءِ ذاتِ الرَّجْعِ," which is the repeated rotation of the celestial bodies and their return to their initial state over various periods. The rotation of the earth around itself and around the sun, the movement of the planets in the solar system, and the perceived rising and setting of the moon, sun, and stars are all movements characterized by "return."

The existence of these returns is a sign of the general return of human beings to life. However, the first meaning holds greater harmony with the issue of the cracking of the earth and the proofs of Resurrection.

"Qawl Fasl" (قول فصل) means a word that distinguishes between truth and falsehood. Here, based on the context of the preceding verses, a group [of commentators] has considered it a reference to the Resurrection, while another group considers it a reference to the Quran, as indicated in some narrations from the Infallibles عليهم السلام.

Indeed, the description of the Day of Judgment as "Yawm al-Fasl" (The Day of Sorting/Decision) is seen in many verses of the Quran.

It is also possible to consider this expression as a reference to the verses of the Quran, which simultaneously give news of the Resurrection; in this way, both interpretations are reconciled.

In a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, he said:

"Indeed, there will soon be a fitnah (tribulation)!" I asked, "What is the way out of it, O Messenger of Allah?!" He replied: "The Book of Allah; in it is the news of those before you, the information of what is after you, and the judgment for what is between you. It is the Decisive Word (al-Fasl), it is not a jest. Whoever among the tyrants abandons it, Allah will break him, and whoever seeks guidance in other than it, Allah will lead him astray!" [1]

[1]: Sahih al-Tirmidhi and Sunan al-Darimi, as quoted in Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, vol. 3, p. 100, and Tafsir al-Maraghi, vol. 30, p. 118.

Then, to console the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers on one hand, and to threaten the enemies of Islam on the other, He adds: "Indeed, they are plotting a plot" (إِنَّهُمْ يَكِيدُونَ كَيْداً).

"And I am planning a plan" and I shall nullify their plots (وَ أَكِيدُ كَيْداً).

"So give the disbelievers a respite; leave them for a little while" so they may see the end of their affair! (فَمَهِّلِ الْكافِرِينَ أَمْهِلْهُمْ رُوَيْداً).

Yes, they constantly devise sinister plans to combat you:

Sometimes they enter through the door of mockery. Sometimes they resort to economic blockades. Sometimes they torture and harass the believers. Sometimes they say: "Do not listen to this Quran," and they make noise and clamor during its recitation so that they might prevail. Sometimes they call you a sorcerer, sometimes a soothsayer, and sometimes they deem you possessed. Sometimes they believe in the early morning and disbelieve by evening, so as to draw a group away with them. Sometimes they say: "Those who surround you are the poor and destitute; drive them away so that we may be with you." Sometimes they say: "At least recognize some of our gods so that we may join you." And sometimes they plot your exile or your murder.

In short, every moment they appear in a different form and every breath in a different guise, to scatter your assembly, to put your companions under pressure, or to eliminate you and extinguish the light of Allah.

But they must know that Allah has willed for this light to become world-encompassing; this is a Divine light that cannot be extinguished by the huffing and puffing of this person or that.

This is the radiant sun of the sky; it does not vanish because bats close their eyes. They plan, and We plan.

"Kayd" (كيد), according to Raghib in al-Mufradat, is a type of resourcefulness (which has a hidden aspect and involves preparing the preliminaries). It is of two kinds: "blameworthy" and "praiseworthy." Although its use in a negative sense is more common, it is nonetheless used in a positive sense as well, such as: كَذلِكَ كِدْنا لِيُوسُفَ—"Thus did We plan for Joseph" (Yusuf: 76).

The meaning of the "plot of the enemies" in the verse under discussion is clear, examples of which we mentioned above; the Quran is full of accounts of their malicious conspiracies and evil plans. But as for what is meant by the "Divine plot" here, some have said:

It is the very granting of respite that eventually leads to a painful punishment, and some have considered it to mean the punishment itself.

However, it is more appropriate to say that it refers to those Graces that encompassed the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the believers, catching the enemies of Islam off guard, nullifying their efforts, and shattering their conspiracies—examples of which are abundant in the history of Islam.

In these verses, He specifically commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: Deal with them with forbearance.

And give them respite, and do not hasten for their destruction; let the proof be sufficiently established against them; let those who have a slight readiness eventually join Islam. Fundamentally, haste is the work of one who fears the passing of opportunities and the loss of possibilities, and this has no meaning regarding the All-Dominant and All-Powerful Allah.

It is noteworthy that He first says: "فَمَهِّلِ الْكافِرِينَ" (Give the disbelievers respite) and then emphasizes it again, saying: "أمهلهم" (Give them respite). One is from the form Taf‘il and the other from If‘al, and the repetition is for emphasis, without the word being exactly repeated, so as not to sound heavy to the ears.

"Ruwayda" (رويدا) is from the root "rawd" (on the pattern of ‘awd), meaning coming and going and striving to accomplish something with gentleness.

Here it functions as a verbal noun and is accompanied by a "diminutive" form, meaning: give them a brief respite [1]. In this way, Allah, in this short sentence, commands His Prophet three times to show forbearance and grant them respite. This is a model for all Muslims to act with patience, endurance, and precision in their affairs, especially when facing powerful and dangerous enemies, and to avoid any kind of haste or unplanned or ill-timed actions.

Furthermore, in the path of propagating the religion of truth, one must always avoid haste, so that all...

[1]: Therefore, "ruwayda" here replaces the absolute object (maf‘ul mutlaq), and in reality, it is as if it were said: amhilhum imhalan qalila. The possibility suggested by some that "ruwayda" here has the meaning of a command—and thus three consecutive commands have appeared—seems unlikely. Although "ruwayd" comes with the meaning of a command as a verbal noun (ism fi‘l), the most suitable for the verse under discussion, given the accusative case of this word, is that it be an absolute object.

...those who have a possibility of being guided may believe, and the proof may be completed against everyone.

As for why this respite is considered small and short, it is either because Islam triumphed over the enemies in a short time and disrupted their plans—they tasted the first blow at the field of Badr, then soon their plans led to defeat at the fields of Ahzab, Khaybar, Siffin, and others, and the pillars of their palace of power collapsed—and before the end of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله life, the light of Islam encompassed the entire Arabian Peninsula, and before a century had passed, it cast its shadow over a major part of the world of that day.

Or it is because the punishment of the Resurrection is also near, and fundamentally, whatever is certain and inevitable is considered near.

In any case, this Surah begins with an oath by the sky and the stars and ends with a threat to the plotting disbelievers and the enemies of truth and reality. In between, there are proofs for the Resurrection in a beautiful and effective manner, and a subtle discourse regarding the supervision of Divine observers over human beings, and consolation and heartening for the believers—all reflected in very short phrases that possess a special decisiveness alongside their subtlety.

O Allah! Turn the plot and craftiness of the enemies, which has increased in our age and time, back upon themselves, and nullify their sinister plans. O Lord! On that day when the secrets and hidden things are revealed, do not put us to shame. O God! We have no power or helper besides You; do not leave us to anyone else.

End of Surah al-Tariq
